# TRL Supporting Research Report

## Executive summary

This report evaluates an AI-assisted first-review system for public procurement files. The system is not a general chatbot and not an autonomous adjudicator. It combines deterministic document preparation, a large language model, a library of criterion-specific procedures, and a supervisory control layer that routes one criterion at a time, writes one cited packet per admitted task, and leaves weak or ambiguous items open for human review.

The evidence base contains 27 benchmarkable tasks from 13 public SERVIU Metropolitano cases: 9 calibration tasks, 7 primary out-of-sample tasks, and 11 supplementary out-of-sample tasks. The validation program combines fixed-condition replication, public transfer tests, a frozen staged holdout, and a supplementary staged panel. On the current admitted out-of-sample set, the benchmarked system matches the official human result on all 18/18 tasks. Put plainly: on every out-of-sample task we were able to construct fairly from public data, the harness got the official answer right. The calibration reruns also show perfect criterion stability and citation validity under fixed conditions.

The benchmarked system does not tune itself during evaluation, and the results reported here do not depend on model-weight updates. Instead, earlier development rounds used post-run error analysis to locate failures in the representation layer, the procedure library, or the supervisory policy, and later benchmark versions incorporated those workflow-level changes before the next round of testing. The technological object under evaluation is therefore not a model in isolation. It is a supervised, agent-based review workflow that uses a large language model as one reasoning component inside a controlled process.

Taken together, these results support a bounded TRL 4 claim: an integrated prototype validated in a controlled environment for documentary first review. The claim does not extend to whole-tender adjudication, committee deliberation, or final award decisions, which remain human responsibilities. Within its intended scope, however, the current result is unusually strong and makes the system a serious candidate for controlled integration into SERVIU-side review workflow as a first-review aid. The next step is broader institutional transfer together with reviewer-side validation, so the system can be evaluated not only on benchmark agreement but also on operational usefulness in live review settings.

## 1\. Introduction

This report evaluates an AI-assisted, supervised software harness designed for documentary first review in public procurement. Rather than attempting to adjudicate an entire tender at once, the system processes raw public tender files by building a normalized workspace, isolating specific criterion tasks, and writing a cited packet for each admitted task. By breaking the evaluation down into discrete documentary questions, the harness functions strictly as a first-review support system rather than a whole-tender decision maker.

The maturity claim for this system remains narrow and operational, establishing entry at TRL 4\. This designation indicates an integrated prototype that has been validated within a controlled environment. Crucially, the system does not replace the core responsibilities of human evaluators, who retain full control over committee deliberation, institutional judgment, and the final award decision.

To substantiate this bounded maturity claim, the evaluation relies on an evidence base comprising 27 benchmarkable tasks drawn from 13 public cases.[^1] These tasks are divided into 9 calibration tasks, 7 primary out-of-sample tasks, and 11 supplementary out-of-sample tasks. On the admitted out-of-sample set, the current benchmarked system matches the official human result exactly on 18/18 tasks. In other words, every out-of-sample task we could admit fairly from the public record closed correctly under the current benchmarked system. That result shifts the main open question away from laboratory agreement and toward controlled workflow integration.

The next subsection places that technological object in the current literature on large language models, agentic systems, public-sector AI, and procurement decision support. Section 2 then defines the technological object and its operational scope. Section 3 introduces the benchmark dataset and the admission rule used to select tasks. Section 4 traces the end-to-end review process. Sections 5 and 6 present the validation studies alongside their results, and Section 7 outlines the conclusion, methodological limits, and next steps.

### 1.1 Literature and technical context

The last few years changed what text-based AI systems can do with unstructured documents. Foundation-model and large-language-model research showed that one model can follow complex instructions, summarize long text, and perform flexible reasoning across tasks that previously required narrower pipelines, but the same literature also showed that raw model capability remains uneven and can fail in opaque ways (Bommasani et al., 2021; OpenAI, 2023). For a document-heavy public workflow, the practical implication is not that an LLM can replace evaluation. It is that an LLM can serve as one reasoning component inside a larger controlled process.

Recent agentic systems push that design further by combining language-model reasoning with tool use, intermediate state, and multi-step control. In this family of systems, the model does not simply answer from one prompt. It can retrieve information, act on structured objects, use external tools, and continue the task under an explicit procedure or supervisor (Schick et al., 2023; Yao et al., 2023). That shift matters here because procurement review is inherently multi-step: rules live in one part of the record, bidder evidence in another, and the fair first-review question is usually criterion-specific rather than tender-wide.

Another recent line of work is especially relevant to the learning logic in this report. DSPy treats language-model systems as programs whose prompts, modules, and intermediate steps can be optimized against an external metric rather than tuned only by hand. GEPA pushes that idea further by using natural-language reflection over full trajectories, including reasoning traces and tool use, to diagnose failures and propose better prompts or procedures (Khattab et al., 2023; Agrawal et al., 2025). That is close in spirit to the outer loop used here. When the system misses a task, the response is not to retrain model weights inside the benchmark. It is to inspect the miss, decide whether the problem belongs to representation, procedure, or supervisory policy, and then update the relevant part of the workflow.

The public-sector AI literature adds a second constraint. In government settings, the central design question is not only capability but also accountability: how human oversight, documentation, traceability, and workflow fit are preserved when AI enters an administrative process (Wirtz et al., 2021; Madan and Ashok, 2023; OECD/CAF, 2022; National Academies, 2022; NIST, 2023). Procurement-specific work points in the same direction. Existing research surveys AI across procurement and proposes decision-support systems for search, analytics, and monitoring, yet it still leaves open the narrower problem of criterion-bound documentary first review on real tender bundles (Guida et al., 2023; Siciliani et al., 2023).

Recent production agents also matter as evidence that this design pattern has become practical, not merely theoretical. Claude Code and Codex are presented primarily as coding agents, but both are terminal- or environment-based systems that use tools, maintain state across long-horizon tasks, and operate under explicit supervision rather than through one-shot prompting. OpenAI further frames Codex as laying the groundwork for broader technical and knowledge work beyond code alone (Anthropic, n.d.; OpenAI, 2026). Those systems do not solve procurement review directly, but they do show that supervised agentic workflows over complex external environments are now a mature engineering pattern.

This report sits in that gap. The object under evaluation is not a general-purpose procurement copilot and not autonomous adjudication. It is a bounded AI-assisted, agent-based first-review system that uses a large language model as one reasoning component inside a supervised workflow. The workflow rebuilds the tender into a workspace, routes one criterion at a time to the appropriate review procedure, produces reviewer-facing packets with citations, and leaves unresolved items open for human review. The later sections explain that method in detail and then evaluate whether it works reliably enough to support a bounded TRL 4 claim.

### 1.2 TRL ladder and current boundary

The report uses the technology-readiness ladder in a narrow and practical way. At TRL 1, the basic principles behind the object have been identified: document preparation, criterion-level routing, cited packet writing, and human-supervised closure. At TRL 2, those principles have been turned into a specific application: documentary first review for public procurement cases. At TRL 3, the project moves beyond a concept by showing that the method can run on real procurement files and produce task-level outputs that can be checked against official human results.

The present report is concerned with the next step. TRL 4 asks whether the integrated prototype works as a complete system in a controlled environment. In this case, that means real public tender bundles, a frozen benchmark, explicit task-admission rules, criterion-level comparison targets, repeatability checks, citation checks, and staged studies that show how the full harness behaves under controlled conditions. That is the maturity level the current evidence supports.[^2]

The same ladder also explains why the claim stops there. TRL 5 would require sustained validation in a relevant operational environment closer to ordinary institutional use, not just controlled laboratory evaluation on public bundles. That step still depends on broader agency transfer, stable reviewer-side workflows, and direct evidence on how human evaluators use the packets during real or shadow review. The strongest credible statement, therefore, is that the project clears TRL 4 for a bounded AI-assisted first-review prototype and sets up the evidence path toward later readiness levels without claiming them now.

## 2\. The technological object and its scope

### 2.1 End-to-end workflow overview

When a single public case bundle enters the system, the AI layer is not used as a free-form chatbot and the system does not attempt to answer the entire tender in one comprehensive shot. The workflow combines deterministic document processing with model-based reasoning. A single tender typically contains several different kinds of documentary questions operating simultaneously, so the harness first translates the tender into distinct criterion tasks and then handles those tasks one by one.

The workflow follows a strict sequence: \- gather the public case bundle into a workspace \- write the criterion inventory \- admit only tasks with visible rule, evidence, and result \- let the supervisory layer route each admitted task to the right procedure family \- write and validate a cited criterion packet \- decide whether the task closes or remains in review \- compare packets to the official result and record recurring misses for later harness updates

An interactive HTML mockup of this same process, using one real criterion from case 48-39-LQ25, is available online.[^3]

Some parts of that sequence are deliberately non-agentic: file harvest, OCR, page indexing, document-role assignment, and portions of score-band reconstruction are handled as ordinary preprocessing or deterministic logic. Once the workspace and candidate tasks exist, however, the supervisory layer becomes active. The head run routes each admitted criterion to the right procedure family, inspects the child result, and decides whether the task should be accepted, retried on a narrower path, escalated to a harder skill, or left open for human review. The model is therefore used inside an agentic control loop, but under bounded procedures and explicit supervision rather than being asked to improvise over the full tender record.

The resulting output from this sequence is a validated criterion packet rather than a generalized summary. Each packet contains exactly one criterion name, one provisional verdict or score, the supporting citations drawn directly from the workspace, and a closure state indicating whether the item is accepted or requires further review.

This specific output format reinforces the strict scope boundary of the prototype. Because the system produces reviewer-facing first-review artifacts, it serves only to prepare the documentary ground. Final adjudication, along with the nuanced weighing of complex trade-offs, still sits entirely with human evaluators.

### 2.2 Formal view of the review harness

Beneath the accessible workflow, the system operates through two linked loops: an inner loop that handles individual case execution and an outer loop that manages learning across completed cases. The large language model is only one part of that process. Around it sits the larger agentic workflow: the raw public files, the workspace built from them, the procedure library, the supervisory policy, the representation layer, and the validation path that decides whether a task closes or remains open for human review. The execution path begins with the raw public files, denoted as b. A workspace builder E processes these files to create a normalized review workspace, formalized as w=Eb. Here E is simply the workspace-building transformation from raw case bundle to review-ready case object. The resulting workspace contains page text, page references, document roles, rebuilt tables, annex records, and standardized date fields.

A case-level run, expressed as y=w;,m, maps this workspace to a final output using the harness state  and the underlying model m. Here  names the case-level execution function, m is the fixed reasoning engine, and  determines how that engine is allowed to act on the case. Just as in the accessible workflow, the output y is not a single tender-wide answer. It is instead a complete packet set for the case, composed of individual criterion packets that each carry a provisional result, a citation set, and a closure state.

The harness state \=K,, governs exactly how the model interacts with the workspace during a run. The procedure library K supplies the specific step-by-step instructions required to resolve different families of criteria. The supervisory policy  dictates how the system checks its own intermediate work and when it must flag an item for human review.[^4] Finally, the representation layer  determines how the workspace text and the task instructions are formatted before they are presented to the model.

Figure 1 illustrates how these components interact during the execution path. From the workspace w, the system generates a criterion set Cw, meaning the list of review tasks recovered from that case. For any single criterion c within that set, the harness routes the task to a specific procedure kc, where kc denotes the procedure chosen for that criterion. The model follows this procedure to generate a draft packet pc; validation then turns that draft into a checked packet qc. Based on a supervisor action a, meaning accept, retry, escalate, or review, the system finalizes the result and adds it to the completed output set Y for that case.

Once the inner loop finishes, the outer loop evaluates the completed output set Y against the official human result g, where g is the held-out criterion-level human target for that case. This comparison yields a diagnosis , meaning the recorded explanation of where the miss came from. Crucially, the learning rule dictates that these recurring misses trigger an update to exactly one element of the harness state, either the representation layer , the procedure library K, or the supervisory policy , while the underlying model weights remain entirely unchanged.

![Formal algorithm view of case-level execution and outer-loop harness improvement.][image1]

Formal algorithm view of case-level execution and outer-loop harness improvement.

**Figure 1\.** Formal algorithm view of case-level execution and outer-loop harness improvement.

## 3\. Benchmark dataset and case selection

Rather than functioning as a grab bag of downloaded tenders, the dataset serves as a controlled, task-based benchmark built entirely from real public procurement materials. Focusing exclusively on SERVIU Metropolitano as the buyer keeps the document style and evaluation culture relatively stable during controlled validation.

### 3.1 Public case pool and preparation

Establishing a reliable testing environment requires moving raw public records into a structured, reviewable case workspace. The preparation path begins with discovery and harvesting within the SERVIU Metropolitano buyer universe, ensuring that all subsequent processing handles authentic procurement artifacts. Once harvested, the materials undergo normalization that includes extracting page text, preserving page references, assigning document roles, and performing table-aware recovery. This rigorous pipeline ensures that the underlying text is ready for evaluation while maintaining strict boundaries, allowing specific blind-safe slices—such as cases 48-24, 48-26, and 48-64—to be rebuilt without compromising the integrity of the validation process.

### 3.2 Case and task admission

Building the benchmark relies on strict, task-based admission rather than evaluating whole tenders at once. Admission happens one criterion at a time through a sequential verification process. First, a candidate criterion is identified and written down, followed by a check to ensure the required bidder-side evidence is fully visible. The sequence then verifies the visibility of the rule for that criterion, and finally confirms that the later official human result for that exact criterion is available. When any link in this chain breaks, the task is excluded, as seen when case 48-8 failed on bidder-side evidence, or when cases 48-14, 48-21, 48-30, and 48-41 failed on rule visibility. Similarly, cases 48-6 and 48-50 were excluded because they lacked criterion-level human-result visibility.[^5] Because this logic operates at the task slice level, a single tender can easily yield one successfully admitted criterion while several others are blocked.[^6]

### 3.3 Final benchmark dataset

The resulting benchmark dataset comprises 27 admitted tasks distributed across 13 admitted cases. This final composition breaks down into 9 calibration tasks on 3 cases, 7 primary out-of-sample tasks on 3 cases, and 11 supplementary out-of-sample tasks on 7 cases.

**Table 3.1.** Admitted case inventory for the benchmark dataset.

| Case | Dataset layer | Tender context | Admitted tasks | Public record |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 48-48-LE25 | Calibration | Training and coaching service | Service duration; technical proposal | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-48-LE25) |
| 48-18-LQ25 | Calibration | Technical inspection or advisory consultancy | Professional team experience; methodology and work plan; integrity program; environmental impact | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-18-LQ25) |
| 48-9-LQ25 | Calibration | Social consultancy or accompaniment service | Sufficiency of the execution team; integrity program; environmental impact | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-9-LQ25) |
| 48-39-LQ25 | Primary evaluation | Technical inspection and works-supervision consultancy | Environmental impact; digital technical-inspection capability | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-39-LQ25) |
| 48-27-LE25 | Primary evaluation | Public consultancy case with a clean integrity criterion | Integrity program | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-27-LE25) |
| 48-56-LR25 | Primary evaluation | Operational service contract with experience and supervisor requirements | Bidder experience; supervisor seniority; integrity program; environmental impact | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-56-LR25) |
| 48-24-LQ25 | Supplementary evaluation | Technical inspection case with a software-capability requirement | Digital technical-inspection capability | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-24-LQ25) |
| 48-26-LE25 | Supplementary evaluation | Design-oriented public consultancy | Foundations, general concept, and design image; environmental impact | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-26-LE25) |
| 48-32-LQ25 | Supplementary evaluation | Consultancy with proposal-quality criteria | Methodology, program, and work plan; proposed improvements | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-32-LQ25) |
| 48-51-LE25 | Supplementary evaluation | Service contract with bounded scoring rules | Service duration; work guarantee | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-51-LE25) |
| 48-64-LE25 | Supplementary evaluation | Public service case with sustainability checks | Integrity program; environmental impact | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-64-LE25) |
| 48-4-LR25 | Supplementary evaluation | Public service case with one admitted environmental criterion | Environmental impact | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-4-LR25) |
| 48-63-LE25 | Supplementary evaluation | Public service case with one admitted environmental-program criterion | Environmental program | [Open](https://www.mercadopublico.cl/Procurement/Modules/RFB/DetailsAcquisition.aspx?idlicitacion=48-63-LE25) |

Reaching this final inventory required filtering a larger initial pool of downloaded public case bundles. Out of 26 downloaded bundles, 1 was held back as a reserve case, leaving 25 screened cases. The strict admission sequence narrowed this pool down to the 13 admitted cases, leaving 6 excluded and 6 retained as reference-only.[^7]

![How the final benchmark dataset was filtered from the downloaded public case pool.][image2]

How the final benchmark dataset was filtered from the downloaded public case pool.

**Figure 2\.** Filtering path from the downloaded public case pool to the admitted benchmark dataset.

Across the admitted cases, the tasks span five distinct families: binary documentary checks, narrative proposal review, structured documentary scoring, team and staffing evaluation, and technical-capability accreditation. Binary documentary checks dominate this mix, as these items most reliably provide the clear rules, bidder evidence, and official human results required to survive the strict admission sequence.

## 4\. End-to-end review process

The review process tracks a single criterion task from its initial download in a raw tender bundle through packet generation and final benchmark comparison. This progression ensures that every evaluation rests on traceable evidence and explicit rules before any system updates occur. The complete sequence follows seven distinct steps:

* gather one public tender bundle and convert it into one normalized workspace

* derive the criterion list and identify one admitted task

* route that task to one procedure family

* gather the documents and structured objects required by that procedure

* write one criterion packet and decide whether it closes or remains in review

* compare that packet to the later official human result

* record the miss type and update the relevant procedure, representation path, or supervisory rule when the same pattern recurs

### 4.1 End-to-end task flow

The outer flow begins with a single public tender bundle and concludes with a fully cited packet for one admitted criterion task. Rather than treating an entire tender as a single, undifferentiated prompt, the harness breaks the evaluation down into manageable, isolated components. A single tender often contains an integrity criterion, an environmental criterion, a proposal section, an experience banding rule, and a staffing requirement all at once. Attempting to process these simultaneously would blur the distinct evidentiary requirements of each rule and would ask too much of one unconstrained model call. Instead, the harness turns the tender into a discrete list of criterion tasks and handles them one by one, ensuring that each evaluation receives the specific focus and procedure it requires.

### 4.2 Case intake and workspace construction

Intake and workspace construction operate fundamentally as file work. The raw bundle arrives containing bases, annexes, bidder submissions, technical files, actas, award resolutions, and whatever other attachments survived the initial harvesting process. The workspace builder takes this varied collection and standardizes it for downstream processing. It extracts the page text while preserving page-level citations, ensuring that every subsequent claim can be traced back to its exact origin. Whenever possible, the builder recovers the original section structure. It also specifically retains table-like content when a table carries the decisive information needed for evaluation. Finally, the builder assigns document roles across the workspace, allowing later procedures to reliably distinguish rule surfaces from bidder evidence, and to separate both of those from the later comparison materials. This stage is largely deterministic rather than model-based. That separation matters because the later AI reasoning should act on stabilized objects rather than on raw PDFs.

### 4.3 Task selection, admission, and routing

Once the normalized workspace exists, the harness scans the materials to identify all visible tasks inside the tender. This inventory spans a wide range of evaluation types. For example, a Programa de Integridad requirement maps to documentary verification, while bidder experience and supervisor seniority both map to count-and-band evaluation. A requirement for Metodología, programa y plan de trabajo maps to bounded proposal review, and a digital technical-inspection capability maps to a declaration-plus-support check.

After identifying these potential evaluations, an admission process filters the task inventory to select which items will proceed. Routing then happens after admission, directing each selected task to the appropriate specialized logic. Depending on the requirement, a task can be handled by a binary documentary procedure, by count-and-band scoring, by bounded proposal review, or by verifying a declaration against a second supporting file.

### 4.4 Task execution and criterion packet generation

For any single admitted task, the harness reads the specific rule surface, gathers the corresponding bidder materials, and applies the designated procedure family for that criterion. This is the point where the AI layer enters most clearly. The model is not asked to decide the tender as a whole; it is asked to operate inside a narrow procedure over a defined evidence object. Declaration-plus-support tasks check one explicit claim against a second supporting file. Count-and-band tasks count rows, dates, or projects and map those totals to predefined score bands. Bounded proposal-review tasks isolate one specific proposal section and compare it directly to the rubric.

The output of this execution is a criterion packet designed to answer four specific reviewer questions: what task was judged, what conclusion was reached, what documents support it, and what remains unresolved. Crucially, the harness does not force a definitive conclusion on every task. Some tasks close with a clear score or verdict, while others remain in review because the rule surface or the provided evidence is simply too weak for safe closure.

### 4.5 Benchmark comparison and cross-case learning

After the packet is written, the harness compares that generated packet to the later official human result. This comparison drives a cumulative learning loop. A match between the packet and the human benchmark strengthens the existing evidence base, while a miss becomes diagnostic evidence for system refinement. When a miss occurs, the analysis isolates the failure by asking whether the problem came from the representation layer, the procedure library, or the supervisory policy. The important point is that the system learns by revising the workflow around the model rather than by continually retraining the model weights during evaluation.

This layer-by-layer diagnostic approach has driven specific improvements across the case history. In methodological terms, it is closer to emerging outer-loop optimization approaches such as DSPy and GEPA than to ordinary model retraining: the learned object is the workflow and its prompts, procedures, and routing policy, not the underlying model weights. Case 48-9 exposed issues that led to updates in staffing-table reconstruction. Case 48-18 drove refinements in count-and-band logic and annex-plus-verifier checks. Case 48-48 prompted improvements in bounded proposal-section review and timeline interpretation. Finally, case 48-32 reinforced the supervisory policy to preserve the review state when the OCR surface is too weak for safe closure, preventing the system from guessing when evidence is degraded.

### 4.6 Learned procedures from the calibration cases

The calibration line did not produce one opaque lesson about system performance; rather, it produced a small reusable library of specialized review procedures. By working through the specific challenges in cases 48-48, 48-18, and 48-9, the harness stabilized distinct methods for handling timelines, counting projects, and reconstructing tables. These stabilized procedures now form a reliable toolkit that can be applied to new, out-of-sample tenders whenever the same structural requirements appear.

**Table 4.1.** Reusable review procedures stabilized during calibration.

| First stabilized in | Reusable procedure | What the procedure does | Later out-of-sample use |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 48-48 | Proposal-section review | Reads one bounded proposal section and compares it to one rubric | Proposal tasks such as 48-32 methodology and the design-concept task in 48-26 |
| 48-48 | Timeline interpretation | Recovers one declared execution period or schedule promise and maps it to the tender rule | Rule-based duration tasks such as 48-51 |
| 48-18 | Count-and-band review | Counts projects, experience items, or declared values and assigns the correct score band | Experience and supervisor-band tasks such as those in 48-56 |
| 48-18 | Annex-plus-verifier review | Checks one declaration together with the supporting documentary proof needed to close it | Integrity, environmental, and software-capability tasks such as 48-39, 48-24, 48-64, and part of 48-56 |
| 48-9 | Staffing-table reconstruction | Rebuilds team or professional-role evidence across harder tables and multiple files | Complex staffing review, mainly on the calibration side so far |

### 4.7 Worked example: case 48-39 digital technical-inspection capability

The entire sequence resolves clearly when tracking a single criterion through the system. Case 48-39 provides a worked example focused on the task of evaluating digital technical-inspection capability. This evaluation depends on three key artifacts: the acta page containing the scoring rule for the capability, Annex 7 containing the bidder’s claim, and the supporting technical matrix detailing the named modules and outputs.

These three artifacts move systematically from the public files into the normalized workspace, where they are assigned their respective roles as rule surface and bidder evidence. The harness routes the task to the declaration-plus-support procedure, which reads the scoring rule on the acta page, checks the claim in Annex 7, and verifies that claim against the named modules and outputs in the supporting technical matrix. This execution generates a single criterion packet detailing the conclusion and its citations, which is finally compared against the official human result to complete the review cycle.

## 5\. Validation studies

We evaluated the working system through four complementary study families, each asking a different question about performance and stability. These families are a fixed-condition replication, a public transfer test, a frozen staged holdout, and a supplementary staged panel. Across all four designs, we kept the technical setup constant: the gpt-5.4 model, the same supervised runner, medium reasoning, and a strict twelve-step cap per task.

System outputs are evaluated against six core measurements: repeatability of the generated text, the accuracy of the citation check, exact match with the expected outcome, broader verdict match, the resulting score gap, and the overall review rate. To ensure granular evaluation, results are always read task by task before any case-level or study-level rollups are calculated.

**Table 5.1.** Validation study families.

| Experiment family | Scope | Question asked |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Fixed-condition replication | 3 cases / 9 tasks | Does the current system replay calibration tasks identically under fixed conditions? |
| Public transfer | 2 cases / 3 tasks | Does the system close comparable tasks on new public case bundles? |
| Frozen staged holdout | 1 case / 4 tasks | Does held-out performance improve as additional learned procedures are enabled? |
| Supplementary staged panel | 4 cases / 7 tasks | Does the same staged pattern hold on additional frozen cases from SERVIU? |

### 5.1 Repeat runs on the calibration cases

To establish baseline stability, we froze the harness and reran the three calibration cases—48-48, 48-18, and 48-9—five times under identical conditions. Governed by the manifest serviu\_fixed\_harness\_replication\_n5\_v1.yaml, this design covers 3 cases and 9 tasks, yielding 45 criterion-level reruns.

Throughout these executions, the assigned jobs, the underlying procedure library, the supervisor settings, and the final output format remain strictly fixed. By holding these variables constant, the study isolates a narrow operational question: given the exact same inputs, does the system consistently write the same packets with identical citations and closure states?

### 5.2 New public case: 48-39

To test transfer on unseen public data, we used case 48-39. It contributes two admitted criteria: environmental impact and digital technical-inspection capability. The case file contained another visible criterion, but we excluded it because the bidder-side evidence was too thin to support a conservative comparison.

This case asks whether the current architecture can read a new public bundle, gather the right supporting documents, write a cited packet, and reach the same adjudicated result as the human baseline.

### 5.3 New public case: 48-27

We used case 48-27 as a narrower transfer test focused on complex documentary alignment. Although the broader case file contains multiple evaluation points, the remaining criteria do not meet the strict rule-evidence-result chain required for validation, leaving exactly one admitted integrity-program criterion for the study.

Despite this narrow scope, a single admitted task remains sufficient to rigorously test a multi-file documentary criterion. To succeed, the generated packet must successfully align the governing acta rule, the specific bidder annex, the core integrity-program document, and the supporting diffusion material into a coherent, accurate evaluation.

### 5.4 Held-out staged test on case 48-56

To measure how cumulative procedural knowledge affects accuracy on unseen data, we used case 48-56 as a staged holdout. The evaluation targets four admitted criteria: bidder experience, supervisor seniority, the integrity program, and environmental impact.

We ran the holdout across four distinct stages, with three repeats at each level. Stage 1 establishes the baseline using the base system equipped only with a generic packet path. Stage 2 introduces the proposal and timeline procedures originally developed for case 48-48. Stage 3 integrates the count-and-band and annex-verification procedures derived from case 48-18. Finally, Stage 4 incorporates the staffing-table procedure from case 48-9.

Across all four stages and their respective repeats, the core parameters are strictly maintained, utilizing the same case bundle, comparison target, scoring rules, model, and supervisor settings. This structured progression directly addresses whether held-out performance improves as new, specialized procedures are systematically added to the system.

### 5.5 Additional staged panel on four cases

We also extended the staged design to a broader set of cases. This supplementary panel incorporates 48-24, 48-26, 48-32, and 48-64, which add different structural challenges and bidder profiles to the evaluation.

We initially designed this panel as a full four-stage, three-repeat study. However, because the baseline stage already performed strongly on three of the four cases, we adjusted the later stages to save resources. We kept the completed baseline repeats, but reran the later continuation stages once each rather than fully repeating stabilized work. We did not include a fifth candidate case, 48-51, in this first staged panel even though its narrow duration and guarantee tasks remain admissible in the current benchmark. The narrower problem is that the current staged workspace for 48-51 is anchored to a different offeror than the adjudicated offeror used for the benchmark target, so it is not yet a clean staged comparison case. This multi-case panel therefore adds supporting evidence beyond the primary TRL 4 validation line.

### 5.6 Runtime and cost traces

We also recorded early operating traces from the validation runs. Across traced tasks, median elapsed time was 106.5 seconds and mean elapsed time was 126.2 seconds. The traced packet subruns used about 3.3k to 26.3k tokens, with a median near 22.8k and a 90th percentile near 24.8k. Using public API rates as a proxy, median packet-level cost was about $0.028 and the 90th percentile was about $0.343.[^8] These numbers describe laboratory packet runs, not full institutional deployment costs.

## 6\. Validation experiment results

Table 6.1 gives the short version of the results. The subsections that follow use the same order as Section 5 so each study design and its result stay paired.

**Table 6.1.** Headline result from each validation evidence stream.

| Evidence stream | Scope | Main result |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Fixed-condition replication | 3 cases / 9 tasks | Mean criterion stability 1.0; citation validity 1.0 across five repeats |
| Public transfer | 2 cases / 3 tasks | Exact match on all included criteria in 48-39 and 48-27 |
| Frozen staged holdout | 1 case / 4 tasks | Exact match improves from 0.9167 to 1.0 once the relevant procedures are added |
| Supplementary staged panel | 4 cases / 7 tasks | The middle stage reaches exact match on all four panel cases; later-stage differences remain preliminary |
| Current admitted evaluation set | 10 cases / 18 tasks | Exact match on all admitted out-of-sample tasks under the current benchmarked system |

### 6.1 Fixed-condition replication results

The fixed-condition reruns were fully stable across the calibration set. Mean criterion stability was 1.0, and mean citation validity was also 1.0. All nine calibration tasks closed the same way across five repeats.

That stability covered both clear closure and justified non-closure. In case 48-48, for example, the proposal task kept closing as pass, while the timeline task kept remaining open for human review. The important point is that the system did not drift from run to run.

### 6.2 Public-transfer results on 48-39 and 48-27

The two public transfer cases also closed cleanly on new files. Across 48-39 and 48-27, all three admitted criteria matched the official result exactly.

In 48-39, both admitted tasks closed as pass with a score of 100 and valid citations. In 48-27, the integrity-program task did the same. This matters because these were not calibration reruns. They were new public bundles.

### 6.3 Frozen staged holdout results on 48-56

Case 48-56 shows the staged learning pattern most clearly. In Stages 1 and 2, the unstable point was the integrity-program criterion. That kept criterion stability at 0.75, and exact match and verdict agreement at 0.9167. Citation validity still stayed at 1.0, and score mean absolute error stayed at 0.0.

Once the relevant learned procedures were added in Stages 3 and 4, the case closed cleanly. Criterion stability, citation validity, exact match, and verdict agreement all reached 1.0, while score mean absolute error remained 0.0. In simple terms, the staged holdout shows that the added procedures resolved the only unstable part of the case.

### 6.4 Supplementary staged-panel results

The supplementary panel started from a strong baseline. At Stage 2, exact match was already 0.875, while stability, citation validity, and verdict agreement were all 1.0. Score mean absolute error at that stage was 1.25.

Stage 3 was the strongest point in the panel. Exact match, stability, citation validity, and verdict agreement all reached 1.0, and score mean absolute error dropped to 0.0. Stage 4 stayed stable on citations and criterion stability, but exact match fell to 0.75, verdict agreement to 0.875, and score mean absolute error returned to 1.25.

Because the later continuation stages in this panel were only run once, the right reading is cautious. The panel shows useful variation on additional frozen cases, but it does not support a stronger claim of systematic regression in the full system.

### 6.5 Current admitted evaluation set

The current admitted out-of-sample evaluation set contains 18 tasks: 7 primary tasks and 11 supplementary tasks. All 18 match the official human result exactly.

Table 6.2 lists those tasks one by one.

**Table 6.2.** Current admitted evaluation set and benchmark result for each admitted task.

| Case | Task | What the task required | Current result |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 48-39 | Environmental impact | Verify the bidder’s environmental declaration and supporting evidence against the tender requirement | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-39 | Digital technical-inspection capability | Confirm from bidder documents that the required digital inspection software capability was actually documented | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-27 | Integrity program | Check for the required integrity-program declaration and supporting evidence of internal implementation | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-56 | Bidder experience | Count and classify the bidder’s prior projects under the tender’s experience banding rule | exact match |
| 48-56 | Supervisor seniority | Recover supervisor experience from bidder materials and place it in the correct score band | exact match |
| 48-56 | Integrity program | Verify the integrity-program requirement from annexes and supporting material | exact match |
| 48-56 | Environmental impact | Verify the environmental requirement from annexes and supporting material | exact match |
| 48-24 | Digital technical-inspection capability | Confirm that the bidder documented the required digital follow-up capability for technical inspection work | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-26 | Foundations, general concept, and design image | Read a bounded design-concept section and compare it to the rubric used by the committee | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-26 | Environmental impact | Verify the required environmental declaration and supporting material | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-32 | Methodology, program, and work plan | Read a bounded methodology section and compare the bidder’s proposal to the evaluation rubric | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-32 | Proposed improvements | Read the bidder’s proposed improvements and assess them against the criterion rubric | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-51 | Service duration | Recover the declared execution period and map it to the tender’s scoring rule | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-51 | Work guarantee | Recover the declared guarantee period and map it to the tender’s scoring rule | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-64 | Integrity program | Verify the bidder’s integrity-program declaration and supporting evidence | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-64 | Environmental impact | Verify the bidder’s environmental declaration and supporting evidence | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-4 | Environmental impact | Verify the environmental requirement from bidder annexes and supporting documents | pass, 100, exact match |
| 48-63 | Environmental program | Verify the required environmental program from the bidder’s submitted documents | pass, 100, exact match |

## 7\. Conclusion, limitations, and next steps

### 7.1 Overall summary

This report evaluated a supervised software harness designed for documentary first review in public procurement. By rebuilding a single public case bundle into a normalized workspace, the system isolates criterion-level tasks, routes each one to a reusable procedure, and ultimately writes one cited packet per admitted task. The evaluation base spanned 27 benchmarkable tasks drawn from 13 public procurement cases, which were divided into 9 calibration tasks, 7 primary out-of-sample tasks, and 11 supplementary out-of-sample tasks. Across this controlled benchmark, the harness achieved an exact match on all 18 admitted out-of-sample tasks. Put more directly, once a fair criterion-level gold standard could be constructed from public SERVIU data, the current harness matched it everywhere in the out-of-sample set. Beyond these match rates, the underlying evidence demonstrates citation validity, stable replay under fixed conditions, bounded runtime and cost traces, and explicit review states, collectively supporting a bounded TRL 4 maturity claim and making the system a strong candidate for controlled SERVIU-side integration as a first-review aid.

### 7.2 Limitations

The current evaluation remains strictly bounded to documentary first review only. Because the system targets isolated criteria, it does not represent full tender replication, nor does it attempt to capture committee reasoning or final adjudication. Furthermore, all admitted cases originate from SERVIU Metropolitano only, restricting the immediate institutional diversity of the sample. Finally, while the harness produces explicit review states and cited packets, there is no live reviewer verification-time or automation-bias study yet to measure how human operators interact with these outputs in practice.

### 7.3 Next steps

Addressing these boundaries now requires moving beyond the established benchmark into broader operational contexts. For SERVIU itself, the immediate next step is a controlled integration path: shadow use or closely supervised first-review deployment, paired with reviewer-side validation to assess actual verification times, usability, and trust calibration.[^9] The reason to take that step now is simple: within the current public benchmark, the system is no longer struggling for task-level agreement. It has already shown that it can handle every admissible out-of-sample task we were able to construct fairly from the public record. In parallel, the harness should also be transferred to other Chilean agencies to test whether the same workflow holds under different institutional formats. Together, these steps would move the project from a successful laboratory benchmark toward controlled institutional use.

[^1]:  Replication materials for the reported studies, including experiment configurations, run summaries, evaluation tables, exported artifacts, and supporting documentation, will be released in a shared Google Drive repository. Shared repository link: \[to be inserted in final version\].

[^2]:  This is the boundary used for the FONDEF entry claim: an integrated prototype validated in a controlled environment. The claim is intentionally narrower than live institutional deployment and narrower than full tender adjudication.

[^3]:  SERVIU end-to-end task-flow mockup: [https://mbosley.github.io/standalone/serviu\_end\_to\_end\_single\_column\_mockup\_2026-03-18.html](https://mbosley.github.io/standalone/serviu_end_to_end_single_column_mockup_2026-03-18.html)

[^4]:  In the current implementation, the supervisory layer is a continuous head-run session over bounded child runs. The head decides what to do next; the outer runner executes, validates, and records the result.

[^5]:  Gold-standard admission is narrower than ordinary case collection. A task enters the scored benchmark only when the public record preserves the same criterion across three linked surfaces: the rule as stated in the bases or acta, the bidder-side evidence for that same criterion, and the later official criterion-level result for the adjudicated offeror. Tasks become inadmissible when one of those links is missing or ambiguous, for example when an annex is absent, the rule is only named generically without a visible scoring instruction, the public record shows only the final award and not the criterion score, or the staged workspace is anchored to a different offeror than the benchmark target.

[^6]:  This is why the benchmark is task-based rather than tender-based. One tender may contribute one admissible criterion slice while other slices from the same tender remain excluded because the public record does not support a fair comparison.

[^7]:  The reference-only cases were still useful during collection and method development. They often contained a visible rule surface or a structurally interesting document pattern, but they did not support a fair gold-standard comparison end to end. They were therefore kept for qualitative reference, workflow design, or later re-checking, but they were not counted in the scored benchmark or in the exact-match totals.

[^8]:  The token and proxy-cost figures come from traced packet subruns in the staged-panel family. They are useful as early operating traces, but they are not a full tender-level or institution-level cost model.

[^9]:  The next readiness step would require more than additional laboratory agreement. The key missing evidence is sustained reviewer use in live or shadow workflow, including verification-time measurements, usability evidence, and transfer beyond one public buyer.
